quote:
modules_to_reload = [
            'browser_manager',
            'network_interceptor',
            'dom_handler',
            'debug_logger',
            'models'
        ]
        reloaded_modules = []
        for module_name in modules_to_reload:
            if module_name in sys.modules:
                importlib.reload(sys.modules[module_name])
                reloaded_modules.append(module_name)
what: The `hot_reload` MCP tool (docstring: "Hot reload all modules without restarting the server") is the codebase's own purpose-built answer to F-120's gap, and it does not work. It never reloads server.py itself (where the actual tool implementations live) or cdp_function_executor.py — the fourth singleton declared in the identical server.py:1297-1300 block as browser_manager, silently excluded with no comment. For the modules it does reload, `browser_manager = BrowserManager()` (line 2997) constructs a fresh instance with an empty _instances dict, discarding every live browser session, _spawn_diagnostics entry, and _proxy_forwarder, and abandoning the running _idle_reaper_task uncancelled (it keeps running, closed over the now-orphaned old self). `grep -rn hot_reload tests/` returns zero matches — no test exercises this tool at all.
why_it_matters: A tool named exactly for "apply my edit without a restart", surfaced in the debugging section, will be reached for by anyone hitting F-120's friction. It silently fails to load the one file (server.py) that contains almost all real tool logic, silently skips a sibling singleton, and — if any browser instance is active when it's called — silently breaks that session (get_instance/navigate/close_instance all report "not found" afterward) while leaving the real Chrome process and idle-reaper task running headless, all while returning a success-looking string. Untested, so this has no regression protection at all.
fix_direction: Either remove hot_reload entirely and document that a full backend restart (kill + reconnect) is the only supported way to apply an edit, or fix it properly: reload every one of the 15 singleton-owning modules including server.py's own dependencies, migrate live _instances/_proxy_forwarders state into the new BrowserManager instance (or refuse to reload while instances are active), and cancel the old idle-reaper task before replacing browser_manager.

what: The module is named persistent_storage.py and its singleton is imported everywhere as `persistent_storage`, but the class itself (honestly named InMemoryStorage) does zero disk I/O — it's a dict behind a lock, module-level singleton at persistent_storage.py:95. This is not an oversight the codebase is unaware of: server.py:1260-1276 (app_lifespan shutdown) explicitly calls `persistent_storage.clear_all()` on every graceful shutdown and logs "Clearing in-memory storage ... cleared", treating it as scoped to the current process on purpose. It's consulted only as a secondary cross-check in list_instances() (server.py:1422-1439), never as a source of truth to restore from.
why_it_matters: Everything tracked here — every browser instance's last-known state — is unconditionally lost on any restart, including every version-driven eviction this branch's own mechanism deliberately triggers (F-120/F-126). There is no cross-backend version isolation mechanism because there is no cross-backend persistence medium at all — trivially "isolated" only because nothing is shared. The concrete cost is misdirected effort: this audit's own brief had to ask "is it actually persistent" because the name says otherwise, and a future maintainer asked to add real durability could easily assume this module is most of the way there when it is architecturally the opposite.
fix_direction: Rename the module/variable to something honest (e.g. instance_registry.py / instance_registry) and drop "persistent" entirely, or — if real cross-restart durability is actually wanted for the version-eviction scenario — replace the backing dict with an on-disk store (sqlite/JSON file under STATE_DIR) and add a matching restore path that server.py's list_instances() checks alongside live memory.

what: DynamicHookSystem.__init__ (line 169) declares `self._lock = asyncio.Lock()`, and create_hook (line 353) / remove_hook (line 409) correctly mutate self.instance_hooks under `async with self._lock`. But add_instance/remove_instance (quoted here, called synchronously from browser_manager._setup_dynamic_hooks and _discard_instance_unlocked on every browser spawn/close) mutate the identical self.instance_hooks dict with no lock — they are plain `def`, so they structurally cannot take the asyncio lock even if intended to. More significantly, the hottest read path — setup_interception and _process_request_hooks, invoked once per intercepted network request/response as an independent asyncio.create_task per event (line 220-223) — read self.hooks/self.instance_hooks without ever acquiring the lock.
why_it_matters: The lock is applied at only 2 of the 6 touch points on this shared state, and the two it misses are the highest-frequency ones (per-request interception can fire many times per second per active browser instance). It currently avoids visible corruption only because CPython dict operations are atomic between await points, not because the design guarantees it — a future change that adds an await inside add_instance/remove_instance, or a burst of create_hook/remove_hook calls concurrent with heavy live traffic, has no synchronization protecting it. The lock's presence gives a false impression of thread/task-safety across the whole class.
fix_direction: Make add_instance/remove_instance async and take self._lock like their siblings; have setup_interception and _process_request_hooks snapshot self.hooks/self.instance_hooks under the lock before iterating, so all 6 touch points share one consistent discipline instead of 2 of 6.

quote:
# Read-only tooling (the `stealth-chrome-devtools` CLI) imports this
        # package to reuse profile helpers without taking over process
        # lifecycle. Honor an opt-out so importing never kills the running
        # server's browsers, deletes their profiles, or wipes PID tracking.
        if os.getenv("STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY", "").strip().lower() in {"1", "true", "yes", "on"}:
            return
        self._setup_cleanup_handlers()
        self._recover_orphaned_processes()
what: ProcessCleanup.__init__ runs `_recover_orphaned_processes()` by default, which loads `~/.stealth_browser_pids.json` (a real file in the developer's home directory, not test-isolated) and calls `_kill_processes_for_metadata(..., recovery=True)` — genuine psutil-based process termination and profile-directory cleanup — for any tracked entry that predates this process's `_init_time`. The module-level singleton `process_cleanup = ProcessCleanup()` (process_cleanup.py:1023) means this runs for real on the mere act of importing the module (directly, or transitively via `import browser_manager`). The comment quoted here is the code's own admission of the hazard. The only mitigation is `STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY=1`: cli.py:35 sets it proactively for the CLI's `doctor` command, and exactly one test (test_singleton_fast_handshake.py:298) sets it — but only in a subprocess env dict, for a child process it spawns. tests/conftest.py, shared by all ~30 test files including test_process_cleanup.py which imports the class directly, never sets it for the pytest process itself.
why_it_matters: Any test run (or REPL session, or unrelated script) that imports browser_manager or process_cleanup in-process, on a developer machine that has ever left stale entries in ~/.stealth_browser_pids.json (e.g. after a crash or a force-killed terminal), triggers real process kills and profile deletions as a side effect of collection/import — not of any test actually calling the recovery code under test. It is silent and conditional (a no-op when the pid file doesn't exist, e.g. fresh CI checkouts), which is exactly the shape of hazard that never shows up in CI and only bites a specific developer's machine at an unpredictable time.
fix_direction: Add `os.environ.setdefault("STEALTH_MCP_NO_AUTO_RECOVERY", "1")` to tests/conftest.py before the sys.path insert (mirroring cli.py:35's pattern), and longer-term move `_recover_orphaned_processes()` out of __init__ into an explicit call the real entrypoint makes deliberately, so importing the module is never destructive regardless of caller.

quote:
browser_manager = BrowserManager()
network_interceptor = NetworkInterceptor()
dom_handler = DOMHandler()
cdp_function_executor = CDPFunctionExecutor()
what: All 15 embedded/ singletons (this block plus persistent_storage, dynamic_hook_system, dynamic_hook_ai, element_cloner, process_cleanup, progressive_element_cloner, comprehensive_element_cloner, response_handler, debug_logger, hook_learning_system, file_based_element_cloner) construct unconditionally at first import, for the lifetime of the interpreter, with no factory function, dependency-injection seam, or reset hook anywhere. Combined with Python's `from x import y` name-binding semantics (each importer gets its own bound reference at import time, not a live lookup), no code path — including hot_reload, see F-121 — can cleanly swap all holders of a reference to a fresh instance.
why_it_matters: tests/conftest.py works around this rather than through it: it pre-sets STEALTH_MCP_CLONE_OUTPUT_DIR before any import can happen specifically because the module-global ResponseHandler()/FileBasedElementCloner() create their output dir at import time (conftest.py's own comment), and individual test files construct their own fresh class instances (e.g. test_process_cleanup.py's `ProcessCleanup()` per test) instead of exercising the shared singleton — which sidesteps the problem for that one class but leaves the real module-level singleton's state (constructed for real the moment any test file first imports it) never reset between tests and never verified clean. The architecture provides none of the isolation the singleton pattern is usually chosen for, and the workaround is inconsistently applied across the 15.
fix_direction: Convert the 15 module-level singletons to explicit factory functions (get_browser_manager(), get_persistent_storage(), ...) with a process-level cache that tests can clear via a single reset_all_singletons() helper called from a conftest.py autouse fixture; this also gives hot_reload a real mechanism to invalidate and rebuild state instead of the current ad hoc global-rebinding dance.

what: _clear_stale_backend's eviction of a stale-version backend calls psutil.Process.terminate()/kill() directly from the new session's proxy process. On the platform this repo is actually developed on (Windows — confirmed via env), psutil.Process.terminate() is documented to map to the Win32 TerminateProcess() API, which does not run the target's Python signal handlers or atexit hooks — unlike POSIX SIGTERM, which process_cleanup.py:187-188 explicitly registers a handler for (`signal.signal(signal.SIGTERM, self._signal_handler)`). This means the stale backend's own FastMCP app_lifespan graceful-shutdown chain (server.py:1250-1277: browser_manager.close_all(), process_cleanup._cleanup_all_tracked(), persistent_storage.clear_all()) likely never runs on eviction on Windows.
why_it_matters: If accurate, every version-driven eviction on Windows abruptly kills the old backend process without giving it a chance to close its own tracked Chrome instances cleanly, leaving that cleanup entirely to the next backend's _recover_orphaned_processes() startup pass — which is exactly the import-time-triggered, opt-out-gated mechanism flagged as fragile in F-124. The new backend's clean start is only as reliable as that recovery pass. This finding is based on documented OS/psutil termination semantics rather than a directly observed eviction-in-progress trace, so confidence is lower than the others in this set.
fix_direction: Before the hard terminate()/kill(), attempt a graceful shutdown first (e.g. POST a shutdown/close request to the stale backend's own HTTP endpoint, or send a CTRL_BREAK_EVENT on Windows via CREATE_NEW_PROCESS_GROUP, which the process already sets at spawn time in _start_server_process) and fall back to terminate()/kill() only after a short grace period times out.

quote:
Enhanced Element Cloner using proper CDP methods
=================================================

This module provides comprehensive element extraction using the full power of
Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) through nodriver. It extracts:

1. Complete computed styles using CDP CSS.getComputedStyleForNode
2. Matched CSS rules using CDP CSS.getMatchedStylesForNode  
3. Event listeners using CDP DOMDebugger.getEventListeners
4. All stylesheet information via CDP CSS domain
5. Complete DOM structure and attributes

This provides 100% accurate element cloning by using CDP's native capabilities
instead of limited JavaScript-based extraction.
"""
what: Three independently-implemented engines all answer 'extract complete data about this element': ElementCloner.clone_element_complete (asyncio.gather over 6 JS-file evaluations, element_cloner.py:464-539), ComprehensiveElementCloner.extract_complete_element (one 216-line monolithic inline JS string, comprehensive_element_cloner.py:62-278), and CDPElementCloner.extract_complete_element_cdp (raw CDP domain calls, cdp_element_cloner.py:33-93). All three are live, each wired to its own MCP tool, and each returns a structurally different result schema (flat multi-key vs. flat camelCase vs. nested snake_case under an 'element' key). CDPElementCloner's own module docstring explicitly claims it supersedes the JS-based approaches ('100% accurate ... instead of limited JavaScript-based extraction'), yet the other two were never removed or rerouted to call it.
why_it_matters: Any bug fix, new field, or behavior change to 'how we extract element data' must be manually triplicated across 3 unrelated code paths using 3 different browser-automation techniques (JS-file injection, monolithic inline JS, raw CDP). This has already happened partially (see F-142): a hang-avoidance rewrite reached only 1 of the 5 sibling primitives. Consumers also get inconsistent response shapes depending on which of the near-synonymous tool names they picked, with no indication which is canonical.
fix_direction: Pick one engine (CDP-native, per the code's own stated rationale) as canonical, reimplement the JS-file and monolithic-JS variants as thin adapters over it or delete them outright, and converge the 3 output schemas into 1. Keep the 16 tool names as thin call-throughs if backward compatibility for tool discovery matters; the response schema changes regardless.

quote:
# Extract styles using element_cloner
            style_data = await element_cloner.extract_element_styles(
                tab,
                selector=selector,
                include_computed=include_computed,
                include_css_rules=include_css_rules,
                include_pseudo=include_pseudo,
                include_inheritance=include_inheritance
            )
            
            # Generate filename and save
            filename = self._generate_filename("styles")
            file_path = self._save_to_file(style_data, filename)
            
            # Create summary
what: FileBasedElementCloner has 8 methods (extract_element_styles_to_file, extract_element_structure_to_file, extract_element_events_to_file, extract_element_animations_to_file, extract_element_assets_to_file, extract_related_files_to_file, extract_complete_element_to_file, clone_element_complete_to_file) that all repeat the identical 4-step shape: call an underlying extractor, call self._generate_filename(prefix), call self._save_to_file(data, filename), then hand-build a bespoke 'summary' dict of a few counted fields. The 2-line filename+save pair is copy-pasted verbatim (only the string literal changes) at lines 112-113, 180-181, 247-248, 308-309, 368-369, 428-429, 490-491, and 541-542.
why_it_matters: 647-line file where roughly 400 lines are the same wrapper pattern restated 8 times. A change to save semantics (e.g. atomic write, error handling on disk-full, adding a checksum) requires 8 edits instead of 1, and the summary dicts already show inconsistent key naming across the 8 copies (e.g. 'file_path' as str vs Path, differing summary field sets) because there is no shared contract forcing consistency.
fix_direction: Extract a single _extract_and_save(prefix, extraction_coro, summary_fn) helper; each of the 8 current methods becomes a ~5-line call site supplying only the prefix and a small summary-field lambda/dict.

quote:
Extract complete styling information using direct CDP calls (no JavaScript evaluation).
        This prevents hanging issues by using nodriver's native CDP methods.

        Args:
            tab (Any): Browser tab instance
            element (Any): Element object or None to use selector
            selector (str): CSS selector if element is None
            include_computed (bool): Include computed styles
            include_css_rules (bool): Include matching CSS rules
            include_pseudo (bool): Include pseudo-element styles
            include_inheritance (bool): Include style inheritance chain

        Returns:
            Dict[str, Any]: Dict with styling data
what: extract_element_styles_cdp's docstring states its CDP-native rewrite exists specifically to prevent hangs, and extract_element_styles (element_cloner.py:28-65) now does nothing but delegate to it. But the other 4 extraction primitives in the same ElementCloner class - extract_element_structure, extract_element_events, extract_element_animations, extract_element_assets - still call self._load_js_file(...) followed by await tab.evaluate(js_code) (e.g. element_cloner.py:152-153), the exact JS-evaluation pattern the CDP rewrite was built to replace.
why_it_matters: This is a fix that landed in 1 of 5 sibling methods and was never propagated to the other 4. If the underlying hang bug is real (the docstring states it as fact, not hypothesis), 4 of 5 extraction tools in ElementCloner - extract_element_structure, extract_element_events, extract_element_animations, extract_element_assets - remain exposed to it, with no tracking issue or TODO marking the gap.
fix_direction: Port extract_element_structure/events/animations/assets to CDP-native calls following the extract_element_styles_cdp pattern (DOM domain for structure/children, DOMDebugger.getEventListeners for events), removing the _load_js_file/tab.evaluate path entirely once parity is confirmed.

what: clone_element_progressive only ever calls comprehensive_element_cloner.extract_complete_element (progressive_element_cloner.py:40-42), whose output is flat (top-level 'styles', 'eventListeners', 'cssRules'). Yet this block, and the same .get('element',{}).get(X) or .get(Y) fallback pattern repeated in expand_styles (93-104), expand_events (141-146), and list_stored_elements (231-248), defends against the nested CDP-shaped schema ('element.computed_styles', 'element.event_listeners') that only CDPElementCloner produces - a producer that is never wired into ProgressiveElementCloner.
why_it_matters: Dead defensive branches for a data source that is never actually connected are a fossil of an abandoned or planned engine swap. Beyond the maintenance confusion, the 'or'/ternary fallback silently returns 0 or {} on a genuine shape mismatch instead of surfacing an error, which would hide a real regression (e.g. if extract_complete_element's schema changes) behind a plausible-looking empty result.
fix_direction: Once F-140's schema consolidation lands, delete the dual-schema .get(...) or .get(...) fallbacks throughout progressive_element_cloner.py and read the single canonical shape directly; let a genuine shape mismatch raise instead of silently returning empty.
